import React from 'react'; import { Card, CardContent, CardDescription, CardHeader, CardTitle } from './ui/card'; import { Badge } from './ui/badge'; import { Separator } from './ui/separator'; import { Tabs, TabsContent, TabsList, TabsTrigger } from './ui/tabs'; import { BookOpen, Calculator, Target, TrendingDown, TrendingUp, Zap } from 'lucide-react'; const AnalysisMethodology: React.FC = () => { return (
{/* Header */}

Keyboard Layout Analysis Methodology

Understanding how Keyboard Layout God analyzes and scores keyboard layouts using advanced metrics based on biomechanical research and typing patterns.

{/* Overview */} Analysis Overview Our analysis engine processes keyboard layouts using the same methodology as the renowned cyanophage analyzer, evaluating 17 key metrics that impact typing comfort, speed, and accuracy.

17 Core Metrics

Comprehensive analysis of typing patterns

Real-world Text

Analysis based on actual word frequency data

Biomechanical Focus

Considers hand anatomy and finger strength

{/* Detailed Metrics */} Effort & Distance Bigram Analysis Rolls & Flow Advanced Metrics
Effort Lower is Better

Measures the physical difficulty of reaching each key based on:

  • • Row penalties: Home row = 1.0x, Top row = 1.3x, Bottom row = 1.2x
  • • Column penalties: Center columns (5,6) = 1.4x penalty
  • • Finger strength: Weaker fingers get higher effort scores
  • • Letter frequency: Common letters weighted more heavily
Formula: Σ(letter_frequency × position_effort) / total_characters
Distance Lower is Better

Average distance fingers travel between keystrokes:

  • • Calculated using Euclidean distance
  • • Each finger tracked separately
  • • Weighted by bigram frequency
  • • Accounts for finger resting positions
Formula: Σ√((x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²) × frequency / total_transitions
Pinky Distance Lower is Better

Specifically tracks pinky finger movement as it's the weakest finger:

  • • Only measures left and right pinky movements
  • • Higher weight due to finger weakness
  • • Critical for overall comfort
 Pinky Off Home % Lower is Better

Percentage of pinky keystrokes that are not on the home row:

  • • Home row is most comfortable for pinkies
  • • Off-home movements increase strain
  • • Includes both up and down movements
Same Finger Bigrams (SFB) Lower is Better

Percentage of letter pairs typed with the same finger consecutively:

  • • Causes awkward finger movements
  • • Slows down typing speed significantly
  • • Modern layouts aim for <2% SFB
  • • Weighted by actual bigram frequency in English
 Skip Bigrams Lower is Better

Finger movements that "skip over" other fingers:

  • • Skip Bigrams: Skip one finger (2+ key gap)
  • • Skip Bigrams2: Skip multiple fingers (3+ key gap)
  • • Creates awkward hand contortions
  • • More problematic on same row
 Lateral Stretch Lower is Better

Uncomfortable stretching motions between fingers:

  • • Index to pinky stretches (columns 3→5, 8→6)
  • • Causes hand strain over time
  • • Particularly problematic for coding symbols
  • • Modern layouts minimize these patterns
 Scissors & Jumps Lower is Better

Awkward finger crossing and jumping movements:

  • • Pinky Scissors: Adjacent fingers with 2+ row difference
  • • Two Row SFB: Same finger 2+ row jumps
  • • Two Row Jumps: Any finger 2+ row movement
  • • Creates fatigue and reduces accuracy
Inward Rolls Higher is Better

Natural finger movements toward the center:

  • • Left hand: pinky → index (left to right)
  • • Right hand: pinky → index (right to left)
  • • Most comfortable typing motion
  • • Fast and fluid movements
  • • Good layouts optimize for high inward rolls
Example: "the", "and", "tion" - flow naturally inward
Outward Rolls Moderate is Good

Finger movements away from center:

  • • Left hand: index → pinky (right to left)
  • • Right hand: index → pinky (left to right)
  • • Less comfortable than inward rolls
  • • Still better than alternating or SFBs
  • • Balance with inward rolls is key
 Trigram Alternation Higher is Better

Three-letter sequences that alternate between hands:

  • • L-R-L or R-L-R patterns
  • • Allows one hand to rest while other works
  • • Enables very fast typing speeds
  • • Reduces fatigue in long typing sessions
 Trigram Redirects Lower is Better

Three-letter patterns that change direction mid-sequence:

  • • Same hand changes direction suddenly
  • • Disrupts natural finger flow
  • • Causes hesitation and errors
  • • Good layouts minimize redirects
Column 5 & 6 Usage Context Dependent

Usage percentage of the center columns (T/Y and G/H positions):

  • • These columns are harder to reach
  • • Lower usage often preferred
  • • But some layouts intentionally use them
  • • Context matters - what letters are placed there
 Analysis Process

1. Text Corpus Analysis

Process large text samples to determine letter, bigram, and trigram frequencies

2. Layout Mapping

Map each character to its physical position and assigned finger

3. Metric Calculation

Calculate all 17 metrics using frequency-weighted analysis

4. Normalization & Scoring

Normalize metrics and generate recommendations based on user profiles

User Profile Recommendations How different user types are weighted in layout recommendations

Developer

  • ↓ Lateral stretch
  • ↓ Same finger bigrams
  • ↓ Skip bigrams
  • Focus on symbol accessibility

Writer

  • ↓ Effort
  • ↑ Inward rolls
  • ↑ Trigram alternation
  • Optimized for prose

Gamer

  • ↓ Distance
  • ↓ Effort
  • ↓ Center columns
  • Quick key access

Casual

  • Balanced approach
  • ↓ Same finger bigrams
  • ↓ Lateral stretch
  • General comfort
 {/* Footer */}

Open Source & Research Based

Our analysis methodology is based on the open-source cyanophage analyzer and academic research in ergonomics and biomechanics. All calculations are transparent and can be verified through our source code.

MIT Licensed Research Validated Community Driven
); }; export default AnalysisMethodology;
